evidence_quotes.quote: Many games require an internet connection to start playing.
The game "The Crew" published by Ubisoft is a driving game with a sprawling open world which I enjoyed without interacting with the online functionalities.
Although the terms and conditions in the box I bought the game in says I bought a license, not a product, I do not believe this is fair.

paragraphs.text: This comment pertains to the concerns expressed by the Stop Killing Games initiative. Many games require an internet connection to start playing. It is my understanding that this is mostly justified partly as an anti-piracy measure and also allows the publishers to make money from an online shop, even if the game itself is almost entirely singleplayer. The game "The Crew" published by Ubisoft is a driving game with a sprawling open world which I enjoyed without interacting with the online functionalities. Ubisoft has however decided to shut down its servers for this game, which renders my physical copy of "The Crew" completely unplayable. It is of course unrealistic to expect Ubisoft to maintain servers in perpetuity, however, Ubisoft could at low cost remove the requirement for a server connection at game start-up, or even allow third-party servers created by enthusiasts to manage online functionality once first-party support ends. Although the terms and conditions in the box I bought the game in says I bought a license, not a product, I do not believe this is fair. A similar story has played out for many other games, and will play out for many more in the future. Please consider legislating against this.
